title: Incidence and prevalence of extensively drug-resistant tuberculosis
subtitle: The estimated number of people living with [extensively drug-resistant tuberculosis](#dod:drug-resistant-tuberculosis) (prevalence) versus new cases each year (incidence).
caption:
Incidence and prevalence of extensively drug-resistant tuberculosis

The estimated number of people living with [extensively drug-resistant tuberculosis](#dod:drug-resistant-tuberculosis) (prevalence) versus new cases each year (incidence).

Between 1990 and 2023, the global count of people living with extensively drug-resistant tuberculosis and the number of new cases each year both show sustained upward movement. The vertical axis measures the number of people, ranging from 0 to 35,000, while the horizontal axis spans years from 1990 to 2023. Two lines trace the data: a blue line labeled "Prevalence" representing the estimated number of people living with the condition, and an orange line labeled "Incidence" representing new cases each year. Both lines begin near zero in 1990. By 1995, prevalence sits around 3,000 and incidence near 2,500. Through the late 1990s and early 2000s, both lines rise steeply; by 2000, prevalence reaches approximately 12,000 and incidence about 9,000. The pace of increase slows after 2005, though both continue climbing. In 2010, prevalence is near 28,000 and incidence around 21,000. From 2010 to 2015, the lines flatten slightly but remain on an upward trajectory, with prevalence hovering just under 29,000 and incidence approaching 22,000. After 2015, both resume a steadier climb; by 2020, prevalence crosses 30,000 and incidence nears 23,000. In 2023, prevalence reaches its highest point on the chart at roughly 33,000, while incidence ends near 24,500. Throughout the entire period, the prevalence line remains above the incidence line, and the gap between them widens over time. The data includes tuberculosis cases in HIV-negative people; tuberculosis cases among people with HIV are classified under HIV/AIDS cases. The most visible pattern is that the number of people living with extensively drug-resistant tuberculosis consistently exceeds the number of new cases each year, and both quantities grow across the three decades shown.
